Quantization:Summary
●Reduce required memory to store and train models
●Projects original 32-bit ﬂoating point numbers into lower precision spaces
●Quantization-aware training (QAT) learns the quantization scaling factors during training
●BFLOAT16 is apopular choice

Performance of LLMs
●
Performance of LLMs is mainly determined by three factors
○
model size (number of parameters N excluding embeddings)
○
dataset size (amount of pretraining data D)
○
amount of compute C (PF-days)
●
A petaflop/s-day (PF-day) consists of performing 10¹⁵ neural net operations per second for one
day (roughly 10²⁰ operations per day).
60
A100 GPU = 312 TFlops = 3e14 Flops = 2.7e19 Flops/day
PFlops = 1e15
PFday = 1e15 × 24 × 60 × 60 = 8.6e19 Flops = 3 A100

Some observations
●
Performance depends strongly on scale (model params, data size, and compute used for
training), weakly on model shape (e.g., depth, width)
●
Perf vs scale can be modeled with power laws
●
Perf improves most if model size and dataset size are scaled up together. Increasing one while
keeping the other fixed leads to diminishing returns
●
Larger models are more sample efficient than smaller models — take fewer steps / data points
to reach same loss
69
https://www.cs.umd.edu/~miyyer/cmsc848o/

Flops per Token
73

Flops per Token
74
•
nvocab : vocabulary size
•
nctx : context length (sequence length)
•
dmodel : model width (embedding / hidden size)
•
dattn : attention projection size (often ≈ dmodel)
•
dff : feedforward (MLP) inner width (often ≈ 4dmodel)
•
nlayer : number of Transformer layers (blocks)

Why 2 FLOPs per Parameter in Forward Pass?
●
FLOP 1 multiply
●
FLOP 2 add
75
Why 2 FLOPs per parameter in forward pass?
FLOP 1: multiply
1.The unit i multiplies its output h(i) by w to send it to
the unit j.
2.The unit j adds the unit i’s contribution to its total input
a(j).
Source:
https://medium.com/@dzmitrybahdanau/the-flops-
calculus-of-language-model-training-3b19c1f025e4

Issues with Kaplan Laws
●
Used same learning rate schedule for all training runs, regardless of how many training tokens /
batches!
●
This schedule needs to be adjusted based on the number of training steps; otherwise, it can
impair performance
●
The resulting “scaling laws” from Kaplan et al., are flawed because of this!
76
https://www.cs.umd.edu/~miyyer/cmsc848o/

Training Computer-Optimal Large Language Models: Hoffman et al., 2022
77
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.15556.pdf

Kaplan Vs. Hoffmann
●
Kaplan et al., 2020: if you’re able to increase your compute budget, you should prioritize
increasing model size over data size
●
With a 10× compute increase, you should increase model size by 5× and data size by 2×
●
With a 100× compute increase, model size 25× and data 4×
●
Hoffmann et al., 2022: you should increase model and data size at the same rate
●
With a 10× compute increase, you should increase both model size and data size by 3.1×
●
With a 100× compute increase, both model and data size 10×
78
https://www.cs.umd.edu/~miyyer/cmsc848o/

How to allocate compute budget
79

How to allocate compute budget
80
TLDR: Chinchilla says to train a compute-optimal model, you
should use ~20 tokens for every parameter
